title: Byeong-Hak Choe
text:
Hello! 👋
I am an assistant professor of data analytics and economics at School of Business at SUNY Geneseo.
My research interests lie in economics of the environment and climate change.

title: Python Basics
text:
Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language. This is a simple Python code:

print('Hello, World!')




In Python, variables can store data of different types without explicitly declaring the type.
For example:

integer_variable = 10
string_variable = 'Hello'
float_variable = 10.5

float_variable

10.5





Python supports the usual logical conditions from mathematics:

# Equals: a == b
# Not Equals: a != b
# Less than: a < b
# Less than or equal to: a <= b
# Greater than: a > b
# Greater than or equal to: a >= b

These conditions can be used in several ways, most commonly in ‘if statements’ and loops.

# if statement:
if 5 > 2:
    print('Five is greater than two!')




A function is a block of code which only runs when it is called.
You can pass data, known as parameters, into a function.
A function can return data as a result.

# Defining a function:
def my_function():
    print('Hello from a function')

# Calling a function:
my_function()




A list is a collection which is ordered and changeable.
A dictionary is a collection which is unordered, changeable and indexed.

# List example:
my_list = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# Dictionary example:
my_dict = {'name': 'John', 'age': 36}

title: Starwars
text:
Let’s analyze the starwars data:
starwars <- read_csv("https://bcdanl.github.io/data/starwars.csv")

title: Starwars
text:
Variable Description for starwars data.frame
The following describes the variables in the starwars data.frame.

films List of films the character appeared in
name Name of the character
species Name of species
height Height (cm)
mass Weight (kg)
hair_color, skin_color, eye_color Hair, skin, and eye colors
birth_year Year born (BBY = Before Battle of Yavin)
sex The biological sex of the character, namely male, female, hermaphroditic, or none (as in the case for Droids).
gender The gender role or gender identity of the character as determined by their personality or the way they were programmed (as in the case for Droids).
homeworld Name of homeworld

title: Starwars
text:
Human vs. Droid

ggplot(data = 
         starwars %>% 
         filter(species %in% c("Human", "Droid"))) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(x = species, y = mass, 
                   fill = species),
               show.legend = FALSE)

title: R Basics
text:
2.1 1. R as a Calculator
R can be used as a simple calculator. You can perform arithmetic operations like addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), and division (/). For example, typing 2 + 2 in the R console will give you 4.

title: R Basics
text:
2.2 Variables
Variables in R are used to store data. You can create a variable using the assignment operator <- (option/Alt + -). For example:


Code
my_variable <- 10


This will store the value 10 in my_variable.

title: R Basics
text:
2.3 Data Types

R has several basic data types:

Numeric: For decimal values like 2.5.
Integer: For whole numbers like 2L (the L tells R it is an integer).
Character: For text or string values, e.g., "Hello".
Logical: For boolean values (TRUE or FALSE).

title: R Basics
text:
2.4 Vectors
Vectors are a basic data structure in R. They contain elements of the same type. You can create a vector using the c() function:


Code
my_vector <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)

title: R Basics
text:
2.5 Data Frames
Data frames are used for storing data tables in R. It is a list of vectors of equal length. For example, to create a simple data frame:


Code
df <- data.frame(
  Name = c("Alice", "Bob"), 
  Age = c(25, 30)
  )

title: R Basics
text:
2.6 Functions
Functions are used to carry out specific tasks in R. For example, sum() is a function that adds numbers together:


Code
sum(1, 2, 3) # Returns 6


[1] 6

title: R Basics
text:
2.7 Packages
R has a vast collection of packages for various statistical tasks. You can install a package using install.packages("packageName") and load it using library(packageName).

title: R Basics
text:
2.8 Help System
To get help on a specific function or topic, use the help() function or the shorthand ?, like ?sum on R Console.

title: DANL Project
section: 2.2 MPG and a Type of Cars
text:
2.2 MPG and a Type of Cars
The following boxplot shows how the distribution of highway MPG (hwy) varies by a type of cars (class) 🚙 🚚 🚐.

ggplot(data = mpg) +
  geom_boxplot(aes(x = class, y = hwy, fill = class),
               show.legend = F) +
  labs(x = "Class", y = "Highway\nMPG")

title: Byeong-Hak Choe
text:
Research

Working papers
1️⃣ Social Media Campaign, Lobbying, and Legislation: Evidence from #cliamtechange and Energy Lobbies
2️⃣ Climate Finance under Conflicts and Renegotiations: A Dynamic Contract Approach


Work in progress
🌟 ‘Hiding Behind a Small Cake’ in an Online Dictator Game: The Way You Hide Matters!, (with Tabaré Capitan (1st author) , Jason Shogren, and Benjamin White).


Book chapters
🌟 Governance and Climate Finance in the Developing World (with Tilsa Oré-Monago).
- In Wu, F., Zhang, D., and Ji, Q. (Eds.), Climate Finance: Supporting a Sustainable Energy Transition, Chapter 7, Springer Nature (Forthcoming, 2024).
